question.text: What does inflation do to the purchasing power of your money over time?

answer.text: It reduces the purchasing power — the same amount of money buys less in the future
It increases the purchasing power — money becomes more valuable
It has no effect on purchasing power
It only affects people who invest in stocks

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Inflation means prices of goods and services rise over time. If inflation is 6% per year, something that costs Rs. 100 today will cost Rs. 106 next year. So the same Rs. 100 will buy less next year — this is called a reduction in purchasing power. **The Trap:** Inflation affects everyone, not just stock investors. Even money kept in a savings account loses purchasing power if the interest rate is lower than inflation. **Cross-Exam Context:** This is also tested in Series V-A (MF Distributors) under inflation risk. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** This is the most powerful argument for investing. Tell your clients: "If your savings earn 4% in a bank but inflation is 6%, you are actually losing 2% every year in real terms." This helps them understand why mutual funds matter.
